mnemonics.title: Scheduling Criteria
mnemonics.content: CUPS TWW - CPU Utilization, Throughput, Turnaround time, Waiting time, Response time - remember as CPU SAVES Time When Working

mnemonics.title: Thread States
mnemonics.content: New Ready Running Blocked Terminated - Remember as NEVER READY TO BE RUNNING, BROKEN, TERMINATED

mnemonics.title: Preemptive vs Non-Preemptive
mnemonics.content: PREEMPTIVE = OS can kick thread OFF the CPU | NON-PREEMPTIVE = Thread stays until DONE or yields

acronyms.usage: Simplest scheduling algorithm, processes requests in order of arrival
Scheduling algorithm that selects shortest burst time next to minimize waiting time
Time-sliced scheduling where each thread gets equal CPU time in rotation
The main processor that executes threads
Operations that cause threads to block waiting for devices
OS designed for real-time applications with guaranteed response times

visualTips.visualization: Draw a circle with 5 labeled points: NEW (top) → READY (right) → RUNNING (bottom-right) → BLOCKED (bottom-left) → READY. Arrows show transitions. NEW goes to READY, RUNNING goes to READY (quantum expire) or BLOCKED (I/O wait), BLOCKED goes to READY when event completes, RUNNING goes to TERMINATED on finish.

Imagine a FIFO queue (like people in line at ticket counter). Threads enter from back (arrival) and CPU takes from front. Round-robin adds a 'second chance' - after time quantum, thread goes to back of line.

Think of a relay race runner (old thread) passing baton to next runner (new thread). The baton contains all information (state) needed to continue - saved in memory (CPU registers). The passing takes time (overhead).

analogies.analogy: Thread scheduling is like a teacher managing students (threads) sharing one whiteboard. The teacher decides which student writes, how long they can write, and when to give others a turn - all to ensure fair sharing and maximum learning.

Time quantum in Round-Robin is like a discussion timer in a meeting. Too short (5 seconds) causes chaos with constant interruptions. Too long (2 hours) means only one person speaks. Optimal (2-5 minutes) balances participation with meaningful work.

Thread pool is like a team of waiters in a restaurant. Rather than hiring a new waiter for each customer (expensive), you have a fixed team always ready to serve. When customers arrive, an available waiter handles them immediately.

Context switching is like a person multitasking between two tasks: writing a letter and answering phone. They must remember where they left the letter (save state), then focus on phone call. The mental effort to switch is overhead.

Starvation in priority scheduling is like a quiet student (low priority) never getting called on in class because louder students (high priority) keep raising their hands. Eventually, without teacher intervention (aging), the quiet student never participates.
